title: Why Economics Matters
body:
Economics matters because most public problems are resource-allocation problems. Housing supply, hospital funding, education budgets, transport investment, and energy transition all compete for finite attention and capital.

It also matters because policy without economics can become performative. Good intentions are not enough if the incentive structure guarantees perverse outcomes.

A person who understands economics is better equipped to see when a policy is likely to work, when it is likely to fail, and when the real issue is distribution rather than total output.

That makes economics one of the most practical lenses for understanding modern society.
keyPoints: Economics explains why prices move and why shortages appear
Policy design requires incentives, not just ideals
Growth and productivity determine long-run living standards
Distribution and fairness remain central questions
Economic literacy protects people from false certainty
furtherReading: Freakonomics by Steven Levitt and Stephen Dubner
The Great Escape by Angus Deaton
Good Economics for Hard Times by Abhijit Banerjee and Esther Duflo
